summary: Small repository hygiene change to `.gitattributes`: bash scripts were changed from the generic `*.sh text` rule to an explicit `*.sh -crlf` rule, with a comment documenting that shell scripts should never use CRLF line endings. No review discussion or PR body was available in the slice.
what_changed: Removed the existing `*.sh text` attribute entry.
Added a comment: `# Never use CRLF for bash scripts`.
Added `*.sh -crlf` to explicitly prevent CRLF handling for shell scripts while leaving surrounding text/binary rules intact.
smart_moves: Made the line-ending policy for shell scripts explicit instead of relying on a broad text attribute.
Documented the reason directly next to the `.gitattributes` rule, making future edits less likely to accidentally reintroduce CRLF-sensitive shell script problems.
Kept the change narrowly scoped to `.gitattributes`, minimizing risk to decompilation code or matching output.
decomp_lessons: Repository metadata can affect reproducibility and developer workflow even when no C/ASM source changes are involved.
Shell scripts in a decompilation project should have enforced LF-compatible endings because CRLF can break execution under Unix-like tooling used for builds, extraction, or automation.
For config-only PRs, prefer small, self-documenting changes that preserve existing attributes for source, assembly, Python, makefiles, and binaries.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modified `.gitattributes` line-ending rules.
Search for build or script failures caused by CRLF endings in shell scripts.
Check whether repository tooling documents required line endings for scripts.
